original_abstract: Text classification is a foundational task in many NLP applications. Traditional text classifiers often rely on many human-designed features, such as dictionaries, knowledge bases and special tree kernels. In contrast to traditional methods, we introduce a recurrent convolutional neural network for text classification without human-designed features. In our model, we apply a recurrent structure to capture contextual information as far as possible when learning word representations, which may introduce considerably less noise compared to traditional window-based neural networks. We also employ a max-pooling layer that automatically judges which words play key roles in text classification to capture the key components in texts. We conduct experiments on four commonly used datasets. The experimental results show that the proposed method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods on several datasets, particularly on document-level datasets.
humanized_abstract: Text classification is one of the basic tasks in Natural Language Processing (NLP). Many text classification algorithms rely heavily on pre-defined features, such as dictionary, knowledge base, and tree kernels. In this work, we investigate a recurrent convolutional neural network (RCNN) for text classification without using any pre-defined features. The RCNN model learns local and global features for each word and then uses a recurrent structure to gather contextual information within a larger span, in contrast to the fixed window size of other neural network approaches and thus potentially with less noise introduced. To extract more important features of words, a max-pooling layer is used. Experiments are done on four benchmark datasets for text classification, and the experimental results show that our proposed model has achieved a new state-of-the-art performance on these datasets, with large improvements on the document-level datasets.
